Congruence Equation

Codeforces 919E Solution

Explanation

Lemma. For $k \not\equiv 0 \pmod p$, the map

\[x \mapsto kx \pmod p\]

is a bijection.

Suppose we iterate over the possible values of $n \bmod (p-1)$ and let

\[n \equiv i \pmod{p-1}.\]

Then from

\[na^n \equiv b \pmod p\]

we obtain

\[n \equiv ba^{-i} \equiv j \pmod p.\]

Thus, for each residue $i$, we obtain the system

\[n \equiv j \pmod p,\] \[n \equiv i \pmod{p-1},\]

where

\[j \equiv ba^{-i} \pmod p.\]

Since $p$ and $p-1$ are coprime, we can apply the Chinese Remainder Theorem:

\[n \equiv ip\Bigl(p^{-1}\bmod (p-1)\Bigr) + j(p-1)\Bigl((p-1)^{-1}\bmod p\Bigr) \pmod{p(p-1)}.\]

Using

\[p^{-1}\equiv 1 \pmod{p-1} \quad\text{and}\quad (p-1)^{-1}\equiv -1 \pmod p,\]

this simplifies to

\[n \equiv ip-j(p-1) \pmod{p(p-1)}.\]

Let

\[y \equiv ip-j(p-1) \pmod{p(p-1)}.\]

We now need to count how many integers $n$ in the range $[1,x]$ satisfy

\[n \equiv y \pmod{p(p-1)}.\]

The count is

\[\left\lfloor \frac{x}{p(p-1)} \right\rfloor + [y \le x \bmod p(p-1)].\]

Repeating this process for all $i \in [0,p-2]$ yields an $\mathcal{O}(p)$ algorithm.

Code

ll binpow(ll x, ll y, ll m) {
    x %= m;
    ll result = 1;

    while (y > 0) {
        if (y & 1) result = result * x % m;
        x = x * x % m;
        y >>= 1;
    }

    result %= m;
    return result;
}

ll modinv(ll x, ll p) {
    return binpow(x, p - 2, p);
}

void solve() {
    ll a, b, p, x;
    std::cin >> a >> b >> p >> x;

    ll ans = 0;
    ll val = 1;

    for (ll i = 0; i < p - 1; i++, val = (val * a) % p) {
        ll j = (b * modinv(val, p)) % p;

        ll n = -j * (p - 1) + i * p;
        if (n < 0) {
            n += p * (p - 1);
        }

        ans += x / (p * (p - 1));

        if (n <= x % (p * (p - 1))) {
            ans++;
        }
    }

    std::cout << ans << '\n';
}
